coredata.dc:title: Highly sensitive GQDs-MnO2 based assay with turn-on fluorescence for monitoring cerebrospinal acetylcholinesterase fluctuation: A biomarker for organophosphorus pesticides poisoning and management
coredata.dc:creator: Deng, Jingjing
Lu, Dingkun
Zhang, Xiaolei
Shi, Guoyue
Zhou, Tianshu
coredata.dc:description: In this study, we demonstrated an assay with turn-on fluorescence for monitoring cerebrospinal acetylcholinesterase (AChE) fluctuation as a biomarker for organophosphorus pesticides (OPs) poisoning and management based on single layer MnO2 nanosheets with graphene quantum dots (GQDs) as signal readout. Initially, the fluorescence of GQDs was quenched by MnO2 nanosheets mainly due to the inner filter effect (IFE). However, with the presence of reductive thiocholine (TCh), the enzymatic product, hydrolyzed from acetylthiocholine (ATCh) by AChE, the redox reaction between MnO2 and TCh occurred, leading to the destruction of the MnO2 nanosheets, and thereby IFE was diminished gradually. As a consequence, the turn-on fluorescence of GQDs with the changes in the spectrum of the dispersion constituted a new mechanism for sensing of cerebrospinal AChE. With the method developed here, we could monitor cerebrospinal AChE fluctuation of rats exposed to OPs before and after therapy, and could thereby open up the pathway to a new sensing platform for better understanding the mechanism of brain dysfunctions associate with OPs poisoning.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Turn-on fluorescent assay
GQDs-MnO2
Cerebrospinal acetylcholinesterase
Organophosphorus pesticides

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of GQDs and single-layer MnO2 nanosheets
Cerebrospinal AChE sensing as a biomarker for OPs poisoning and treatment
Mechanistic investigation on MnO2-nanosheet-based fluorescent assay for AChE activity
Fluorescent sensing of AChE in cerebral spinal fluid
Monitoring the level of AChE in CSF as a biomarker for OPs poisoning and treatment

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research is financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (grants No. 21277048, 21505046 and 21675053), the China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (No. 2016M590336) and the “Chenguang Program” funded by Shanghai Education Development Foundation and Shanghai Municipal Education Commission (No. 15CG21).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A) Fluorescence spectra and photographs (Inset) of GQDs (0.3 mg/mL) without (vial 1) and with (vial 2) the addition of single layer MnO2 nanosheets (120 μM) (λex = 345 nm) (UV lamp, λex = 365 nm); B) Normalized fluorescence excitation (red curve) and emission (black curve) spectra of GQD and UV–vis absorption spectrum of MnO2 (blue curve); C) TEM and HR-TEM (Inset) image of GQDs-MnO2 architecture. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

UV–vis spectra (A) and Fluorescent emission spectra (B) with photographs (Inset) of the dispersion of GQDs-MnO2 architecture before (vial 1, black curve) and after (vial 2, red curve) the addition of ATCh with the presence of AChE (20 μL, 1.0 U); TEM image of GQDs-MnO2 architecture before (C) and after (D) the addition of ATCh with the presence of AChE (20 μL, 1.0 U). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

A) Fluorescent spectra of GQDs-MnO2 architecture after the addition of ATCh with presence of different activities of AChE (from 1.0 to 800 mU) at the time point of 20 min; B) Plot of (F− F 0 )/F 0 versus C AChE. Error bars indicate standard deviations (n = 3).

A) Relative signal intensity (F–F 0)/F 0 of GQDs-MnO2 architecture with the presence of other enzymes (0.4 U/mL) and AChE (0.4 U/mL); B) Relative signal intensity (F–F 0)/F 0 of GQDs-MnO2 architecture with the presence of various reductive species (10 μM) and AChE (0.4 U/mL). C) Fluorescence spectra of GQDs-MnO2 architecture dispersion with the presence of aCSF (black curve), CSF without (red curve) and with oxygen treated (blue curve). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Typical fluorescence spectra of GQDs-MnO2 architecture dispersion with the presence of aCSF (black curve), oxygen treated CSF without (blue curve) and with (red curve) the presence of ATCh (100 μM). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Typical fluorescence spectra of GQDs-MnO2 architecture dispersed in aCSF (black curve), oxygen treated CSF without (blue curve) and with (red curve) the addition of ATCh (100 μM). A) Rat samples exposed to parathion; B) Rat samples exposed to parathion with pretreatment of pralidoxime (PAM-1 group); C) Rat samples exposed to parathion with aftertreatment of pralidoxime (PAM-2 group); D) Relative signal intensity (F–F 0)/F 0 of GQDs-MnO2 architecture as indicated in Figs. 5 and 6 A, B, C. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
A) Schematic illustrations of enzymatic interaction. B) Schematic illustration of GQDs-MnO2 based assay with turn-on fluorescence for cerebrospinal AChE activity as a biomarker for OPs poisoning.
Concentrations of AChE in different rat samples.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: n = Number of rats having tested.

ce:article-footnote.ce:note-para: This paper has been recommended for acceptance by Baoshan Xing.

head.ce:title: Highly sensitive GQDs-MnO2 based assay with turn-on fluorescence for monitoring cerebrospinal acetylcholinesterase fluctuation: A biomarker for organophosphorus pesticides poisoning and management

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Ecological and Environmental Sciences, East China Normal University, 500 Dongchuan Road, Shanghai 200241, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Ecological and Environmental Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering, East China Normal University, 500 Dongchuan Road, Shanghai 200241, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, we demonstrated an assay with turn-on fluorescence for monitoring cerebrospinal acetylcholinesterase (AChE) fluctuation as a biomarker for organophosphorus pesticides (OPs) poisoning and management based on single layer MnO2 nanosheets with graphene quantum dots (GQDs) as signal readout. Initially, the fluorescence of GQDs was quenched by MnO2 nanosheets mainly due to the inner filter effect (IFE). However, with the presence of reductive thiocholine (TCh), the enzymatic product, hydrolyzed from acetylthiocholine (ATCh) by AChE, the redox reaction between MnO2 and TCh occurred, leading to the destruction of the MnO2 nanosheets, and thereby IFE was diminished gradually. As a consequence, the turn-on fluorescence of GQDs with the changes in the spectrum of the dispersion constituted a new mechanism for sensing of cerebrospinal AChE. With the method developed here, we could monitor cerebrospinal AChE fluctuation of rats exposed to OPs before and after therapy, and could thereby open up the pathway to a new sensing platform for better understanding the mechanism of brain dysfunctions associate with OPs poisoning.
A GQDs-MnO2 based assay with turn-on fluorescence was developed for monitoring AChE fluctuation in CSF samples of rats as a biomarker for OPs poisoning and clinical treatment.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, considerable attention has been drawn in organophosphorus compounds (OPs) which are widely used as pesticides in modern agriculture for protecting crops or as chemical nerve agents in terrorist attacks or military activities (Dou et al., 2015; Sara et al., 2016; Tang et al., 2016). Because of their recognized toxicity to non-target species including human, some of them have been severely restricted or banned; as a result, the environmental levels of OPs have fallen over time (Konradsen et al., 2003; Yen et al., 2011). However, due to their resistance to natural breakdown processes, many can be still be found as persistent contaminates in a wide range of ecosystem compartments, including soils, rivers, as far as the deep oceans, posing a great danger to people's daily life (Bondarenko et al., 2004; Chen et al., 2010). According to the literature, the number of intoxications with OPs is estimated at some 3,000,000 per year, and the number of deaths and casualties induced by OPs poisoning is high to 300,000 per year (Eyer, 2003; Yi et al., 2013). Therefore, the development of a direct, sensitive and selective method for monitoring biologically important species that can be used as a biomarker for estimating the potential adverse effects of OPs poisoning and making adaptive protocol is of great importance in early clinical diagnosis and therapy.

Early efforts have already demonstrated that, the acute toxicity of an exposure to these OP chemicals may stem from capacity to inhibit the activity of acetylcholinesterase (AChE), which plays curial roles in central and peripheral nervous system (Ray, 1998; Zimmerman and Soreq, 2006). The inactivation of cerebrospinal AChE leads to the accumulation of the neurotransmitter acetylcholine (ACh) and subsequent impairment of numerous body functions. Therefore, cerebrospinal AChE which reflects the metabolic and pathological states of the central nervous system more directly could be regarded as an appropriate indicator for the degree of OP poisoning (Sarah et al., 2007; Valentin et al., 2016), and monitoring of cerebrospinal AChE fluctuation is beneficial for making treatment strategy to OPs exposure related brain dysfunction.

So far, many efforts have been made for AChE activity detection, including colorimetric assays (Sun et al., 2011; Zhang et al., 2016), chemiluminescence sensors (Sabelle et al., 2002), or electrochemical methods (Chen et al., 2012; Ge et al., 2013) and so forth. Among these strategies, fluorescent probes working in “turn-on” mode are particularly attractive, since the enhanced fluorescence nature of the signal transduction affords a much better signal-to-noise ratio for the sensing scheme and avoids false positive signals to a much greater extent (Huang et al., 2013; Kang et al., 2016; Long et al., 2015). In early works, the application of this kind of method to AChE detection has already been extended. For example, the conjugated polymer with enhanced fluorescence was developed, which could realize continuous AChE detection easily, however, such assay methods always showed low sensitivity, even by tuning the ensemble of polymeric monomer rationally (Feng et al., 2007; Zhang et al., 2011). Switch on fluorometric assay based on organic molecular was also developed for AChE detection with high sensitivity and selectivity, however, the synthesis process was time-consuming and complex (Cui et al., 2011; Peng et al., 2009). In 2012, based-on RB-AuNPs, colorimetric and fluorescent dual sensing mode was successfully applied for cerebrospinal AChE detection (Liu et al., 2012). Although the dye molecule (RB) was small and nontoxic, they exhibited disadvantages of rapid bleaching, the photostability of which need to be improved. Notwithstanding the high performance of the mentioned methods above, the pressing need for monitoring cerebrospinal AChE fluctuation and further used as a biomarker for OPs poisoning and management substantially necessitate great improvements of the proposed method to be theoretically simple, less technically demanding and with high analytical performance and stability.

Recently, luminescent graphene quantum dots (GQDs) emerge as alternative or complementary tools to the organic fluorophores currently used in various fields. Compared with traditional fluorescent substances, they have many unique properties, such as high fluorescence, stable photoluminescence, low toxicity, and excellent biocompatibility, which are very promising for biosensing and imaging (Li et al., 2012; Liu et al., 2013; Zhu et al., 2011). Moreover, as one kind of redox active 2D nanomaterials, single-layer MnO2 nanosheets has greatly evoked extensive research interests, because the wide absorption band, strong oxidation activity, good biocompatibility of which make it appropriate to establish “turn-on” fluorescent sensing platform for the detection of many important reductive species (Deng et al., 2011; Zhang et al., 2014; Zhao et al., 2014). However, as far as we know, relying on the universal oxidation ability of MnO2 nanosheets, these methods could be easily affected by various factors including coexistent reductive species or environmental effects, such as oxygen during sampling, detecting process, and are therefore subjected to some limitations for in vivo applications (He et al., 2015; Zhai et al., 2014).

In this study, we demonstrated a GQDs-MnO2 based assay with stable turn-on fluorescence, which could overcome the problems inherent in the existing methods mentioned above and realized cerebrospinal AChE activity determination as a biomarker for OPs poisoning of rat directly. The design rationale for this assay was illustrated in Scheme 1 and explained as follows: (1) The MnO2 nanosheets served as a nanoquencher towards luminescent graphene quantum dots (GQDs) to form GQDs-MnO2 architecture with very weak fluorescence; (2) Acetylthiocholine (ATCh), is an analogue of acetylcholine, and it can be easily hydrolyzed to generate thiocholine (TCh, a thiol-containing compound with reducibility) in the presence of AChE; the redox reaction between MnO2 and TCh would next occur, leading to the destruction of the GQDs-MnO2 nanosheets architecture, and finally the release of luminescent GQDs into solution. This process eventually resulted in the increase of the fluorescence emitted from luminescent GQDs, which provided a straightforward basis for the sensing of AChE with turn-on fluorescence. Since the reductive species produced from the enzymatic interaction were added adscititiously, the endogenous interference and external environmental fluctuations could be strictly controlled, which could be further applied for in vivo monitoring cerebrospinal AChE fluctuation in OPs poisoning and management of rat samples.

Acetylcholinesterase (AChE) and acetylthiocholine (ATCh) was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Co. Ltd. Glucoamylase (GA), cellulase, horseradish peroxidase (HRP), lipase (LP), glutathione, parathion, tetramethylammonium (TMA), ascorbic acid (AA), dopamine (DA), cysteine (Cys), homocysteine (Hcys) and glutathione (GSH) were purchased from Aladdin (Shanghai, China). Manganese chloride tetrahydrate (MnCl2·4H2O), citric acid and sodium pyrophosphate (PPi) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All of the chemicals were of at least analytical grade and used without further purification.

Artificial cerebrospinal fluid (aCSF) was prepared as a buffer solution by mixing NaCl (126 mM), KCl (2.4 mM), KH2PO4 (0.5 mM), MgCl2 (0.85 mM), NaHCO3 (27.5 mM), Na2SO4 (0.5 mM) and CaCl2 (1.1 mM) into Milli-Q water, and concentrated HCl was used to adjust pH to 7.4.

Fluorescent spectroscopy was measured on a Hitachi F-7000 spectrometer (Hitachi Co. Ltd., Japan) and colorimetric measurements were recorded by an UV–vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu UV-1800 spectrophotometer, Japan). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) measurements were performed using a JEM 2010 (JEOL, Japan). Atomic force microscopic (AFM) images were taken using a Nanoscope IIIa multimode atomic force microscope (Digital Instruments, India).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of GQDs and single-layer MnO2 nanosheets
ce:section.ce:para: GQDs were prepared as reported previously (Liu et al., 2013). In a typical synthesis, the mixture of citric acid (0.5 g) and glutathione (0.15 g) were put into a 5 mL crucible and heated to 240 °C using a tube furnace for 1 h. Then, the as-prepared dark brown powder was dissolved into Milli-Q ultrapure water and the solution was purified by silica gel column chromatography with 0.01 M HCl solution as the developing solvent. After removing the HCl by reduced pressure distillation, the purified GQDs solution was stored in the dark at room temperature.

Briefly, 12 mL of tetramethylammonium (TMA, 1.0 M in H2O) was first mixed with 2 mL of H2O2 (30 wt % in H2O) and then the mixture was added into 10 mL of the aqueous solution of 0.593 g of MnCl2·4H2O (99.99%) within 15 s (Zhai et al., 2014). Subsequently, the color of the mixture was changed from colorless to dark brown. After stirring vigorously overnight, the obtained single-layer MnO2 nanosheets were then centrifuged and washed with water several times.

A fluorescent sensing for AChE was performed under the following procedures. A total of 20 μL of AChE with different activities ranging from 0.001 to 0.8 U was added into the mixture (pH 7.4, 1.0 mL) containing aCSF (100 μL), ATCh (100 μM), MnO2 (87.5 μM), pyrophosphate ion (PPi) (100 μM) and GQD (0.3 mg/mL). The resulting mixtures were incubated for 20 min at 37 °C and then its photoluminescence spectroscopy was measured. Note that, to avoid the flocculation of MnO2 colloid induced by the presence of metal cation Mn2+ during the enzymatic reaction, highly-charged PPi was added to the reaction system to coordinate the Mn2+ and enhance the electrostatic repulsion among negative-charged MnO2 nanosheets (Zhai et al., 2014).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cerebrospinal AChE sensing as a biomarker for OPs poisoning and treatment
ce:section.ce:para: CSF samples preparation was performed with the procedures reported in previous literature (Liu et al., 2012). Adult male Sprague-Dawley rats (300–350 g, obtained from Slrc animal laboratory, Shanghai) were housed on a 12:12 h light-dark schedule with food and water ad libitum. All procedures involving animals were in accordance with the Animal Ethics Committee in East China Normal University. For the OPs treated rats, parathion were dissolved in physiological saline and given by intraperitoneal injection at a dose of 1 mg/kg, and meanwhile the normal rats were treated with physiological saline as controls. After 1 h, each mouse was anaesthetized with 0.3 g/kg chloral hydrate, the CSF samples were collected using a microinjector. Therapeutic rats were divided into two groups: for pretreatment group, the pralidoxime (PAM, 0.6 mL/kg, referenced to the manual of application) were infused 20 min before parathion (PAM-1 group), while for aftertreatment group, PAM were infused 1 h after parathion (PAM-2 group). After 1 h, each mouse was anaesthetized with 0.3 g/kg chloral hydrate, and the CSF samples were collected using a microinjector. The CSF samples were then centrifuged at 1000 rpm for 5 min and prepared for measurements. For fluorescent sensing of AChE in the cerebrospinal fluid, a reaction mixture (pH 7.4, 1.0 mL) containing aCSF (100 μL), ATCh (100 μM), MnO2 (87.5 μM), PPi (100 μM) and GQD (0.3 mg/mL) was first prepared. After removing the reductive species by oxygen, the collected CSF (20 μL) was added into the reaction mixture (1.0 mL) and the resulting mixture was first incubated in a 37 °C water bath for 20 min and the photoluminescence spectrum of the reaction mixture was recorded.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanistic investigation on MnO2-nanosheet-based fluorescent assay for AChE activity
ce:section.ce:para:
Initially, the morphology of MnO2 nanosheets and luminescent GQDs were characterized by Transmission Electron Microscope (TEM) and atomic force microscopy (AFM) measurements. As shown in Fig. S1, the obtained MnO2 nanosheets and GQDs presented obvious single-layer structure, with a thickness of about 0.9 nm and 1.0 nm, respectively. The GQDs were well dispersed with a particle size of about 3.5 ± 1.4 nm and emitted strong blue fluoresce at 420 nm (Fig. 1 A, vial 1, red curve). However, with the presence of single layer MnO2 nanosheets, the blue fluorescence of GQDs quenched clearly (Fig. 1A, vial 2, black curve). The quenching mechanism of GQDs by MnO2 was further explored. Since MnO2 exhibited an intense and broad absorption ranging from 200 nm to 600 nm (Fig. 1B, blue curve) overlapped with and the excitation (345 nm) and emission (420 nm) spectra of GQDs (red and black curves), the MnO2 nanosheets-induced fluorescence suppression of GQDs was firstly considered to stem from the inner filter effect (IFE) (Wang et al., 2015; Yan et al., 2015). The IFE was then corrected on the basis of the cell geometry (Fig. S2A) used in the fluorescent measurements and the absorption characteristics of the mixture of MnO2 nanosheets and GQDs with eq (1) (Gauthier et al., 1986).
(1)
F
c o r
F
o b s d
=
2.3 d
A
e x
1 −
10
− d
A
e x
10
g
A
e m
2.3 s
A
e m
1 −
10
− s
A
e m

Where, Fobsd refers to the observed fluorescence intensity and Fcor is the corrected fluorescence intensity by removing IFE from Fobsd (λem = 420 nm); Aex and Aem represent the absorbance at the excitation wavelength (λex = 345 nm) and emission wavelength (λem = 420 nm), respectively; s is the thickness of the excitation beam (i.e., 0.10 cm in this study), g is the fixed distance from the edge of the excitation beam to the edge of the cuvette (i.e., 0.40 cm in this case), and d is the width of the cuvette (i.e., 1.00 cm in this case).

Table S1 summarizes the absorbance and fluorescence intensity of GQDs at different concentrations of MnO2 nanosheets. The correction factor (CF) of IFE at each concentration of MnO2 nanosheets shown in Table S1 was generated from eq (1). The suppressed efficiency for the totally observed and the corrected (i.e., after removing IFE) was demonstrated in Fig. S2B, from which we found that the maximum suppressed efficiency of IFE for MnO2 nanosheets towards GQDs was as high as 70% of total suppressed efficiency, suggesting that the suppressive effect mainly comes from the IFE rather than single Förster resonant energy transfer (FRET) or photo induced electron transfer (PET) reported in those MnO2 nanosheets based fluorescent assay before (He et al., 2015; Wang et al., 2013). Furthermore, the acquisition of the GQDs-MnO2 architecture was observed by TEM and HR-TEM, which clearly show that the GQDs are well interspersed on the surface of MnO2 nanosheets (Fig. 1C).

Subsequently, the concentration of MnO2 nanosheets was rationally optimized to establish the fluorescent sensing platform for AChE detection with high sensitivity and low background, as shown in Fig. S3. The fluorescence intensity of GQDs decreases with increasing concentration of MnO2 nanosheets in the solution. When the concentration of MnO2 nanosheets was higher than 87.5 μM (calculated from the absorbance of the colloidal suspension of MnO2 nanosheets at 380 nm with the molar extinction coefficient of 9.6 × 103 M−1cm−1) (Kai et al., 2008), the fluorescence of GQDs decreased less sensitively as compared with the case when a low concentration of MnO2 nanosheets was used. However, with the presence of low concentration of MnO2 nanosheets, the fluorescence background would also affect the sensitivity. As a result, we optimized the concentration of MnO2 nanosheets at 87.5 μM to realize sensitive AChE determination with turn-on fluorescence, as demonstrated below.

As displayed in Fig. 2 A, the addition of ATCh with the presence of 1.0 U AChE into artificial cerebral spinal fluid (aCSF) dispersion containing GQDs-MnO2 architecture lead to dissociation of the dark brown colloid (vial 1) into a clear solution (vial 2), accompanied by an obvious decrease of the absorption peak at 380 nm in the UV–vis spectra (Fig. 2A, red arrow). Meanwhile, such a procedure also leads to a change in the fluorescence of GQDs-MnO2 architecture; the fluorescent intensity at 420 nm, which was ascribed to the emission of GQDs, increases (Fig. 2B, red arrow). This phenomenon could be due to the AChE-catalyzed hydrolysis of ATCh to produce the enzymatic product TCh with reducibility, leading to the redox reaction which converted MnO2 nanosheets into Mn2+. During this redox reaction, TCh was oxidized to generate thiocholine disulfide (RSSR) as shown in eq (2).
(2)
MnO
2
+ 2 RSH +
2 H
+
Mn
2 +
+ RSSR +
4 H
2
O

This result could also be observed by the disappearance of MnO2 nanostructure and appearance of released GQDs in the TEM image of GQDs-MnO2 architecture after enzymatic interaction (Fig. 2C and D). With the destruction of MnO2 nanosheets, the IFE between MnO2 and GQDs diminished gradually. The increased fluorescent intensity of GQDs at 420 nm (F 420) was closely associated with the degree of the destruction of the MnO2 nanosheets caused by AChE and, as a consequence, could be used for fluorescence assay of AChE activity as a biomarker for OPs poisoning and management.

To ensure the sensitivity of our AChE probe with turn-on fluorescence, the concentration of the enzymatic substrate was optimized firstly. As shown in Fig. S4, when the concentration of ATCh was higher than 100 μM, the fluorescence recovery of GQDs reached maximum and was no longer changed, while high concentration of substrate may bring negative influence on sensitive enzyme activity detection, the concentration of ATCh was rationally optimized at 100 μM. The influence of the incubation time was demonstrated in Fig. S5. The fluorescent intensity of released GQDs increased linearly from 0 min to 18 min, and become stable after 20 min, which means the enzymatic reaction has been almost completed. Hence, we choose 20 min as reaction time for adequately reaction. Moreover, as shown in Fig. S6, when the temperature was between 37 and 40 °C, the enzymatic reaction exhibited high catalytic efficiency. According to the literatures, the body temperature of rats was almost 37 °C (Eger and Johnson, 1987; Kil et al., 1996). As a result, for cerebrospinal AChE detection, we chose 37 °C as optimal temperature for enzymatic reaction.

Under the optimal conditions employed here, we next evaluated the sensitivity of our method. Different activities of AChE were added into buffer solution with 100 μM ATCh at 37 °C for 20 min. After that, the mixtures were added into the dispersion of GQDs-MnO2 architecture. As depicted in Fig. 3 A, (F− F
0
)/F
0 (420 nm) values of GQDs increased with increasing AChE activity linearly range from 0.001 to 0.2 U/mL ((F− F
0
)/F
0 = 0.0221C (mU/mL) + 0.0194, R2 = 0.989) (Fig. 3B, inset), and the detection limit of this method was calculated to be 0.00037 U/mL based on the 3σ rule, which was 1–2 orders of magnitude lower than most of the existing fluorescent methods (Table S2), thus again confirming the turn-on fluorescent strategy with high sensitivity developed in this study is able to realize in vivo AChE detection.

As is known that the brain system is very complex, it requires the established methods with high analytical performance not only in sensitivity but also more especially in selectivity (Huang et al., 2015). To investigate the selectivity of our method, different kinds of species that may possibly be considered as interference for AChE sensing were studied. First, other enzymes such as glucoamylase (GA), cellulase, horseradish peroxidase (HRP), lipase (LP) and alkaline phosphatase (ALP) with the concentration as high as 0.4 U/mL were tested. As shown in Fig. 4 A, the addition of 100 μM ATCh with the presence of the enzymes did not exhibit a significant response, either in the color or the fluorescence intensity (Fig. S7B), which substantially suggested that these enzymes and proteins did not interfere with the AChE assay developed in this study. In addition, the selectivity against reductive species which may coexist in CSF such as glutamate (GSH), dopamine (DA), cysteine (Cys), homocysteine (Hcys), and ascorbic acid (AA) were also investigated. However, as could be seen from Fig. 4B and Fig. S7, 10 μM of these reductive species lead to the changes both in the color and the fluorescence intensity of the resulting mixtures, suggesting that our method was also responsive to GSH, DA, AA to some extent, causing potential interference toward the AChE sensing.

Nevertheless, our control experiments with oxygen flow eventually ruled out such interference in CSF. As we mentioned above, for reductive species determination in rat brain previously, the microdialysis samples always need to be detected as soon as possible to cancel out the oxygen influence. While this ‘‘oxidation’’ can certainly be a disadvantage in certain applications, it is of key importance for our sensing strategy here. As shown in Fig. 4C, direct addition of CSF (red curve) into the mixture lead to an increase in the fluorescent intensity of GQDs compared with separate addition of aCSF (black curve). However, for oxygen treated CSF, the resulting mixture did not show an obvious fluorescence difference with those achieved by aCSF (blue curve), indicating the influence of all interferences (reductive speices) in CSF was already canceled out. After this process, the turn-on fluorescence of oxygen treated CSF was only produced from the enzymatic product hydrolyzed from the adscititious enzymatic substrate selectively. This property, along with the good linearity, substantially enables the utilization of GQDs-MnO2 architecture for the sensing of cerebrospinal AChE activity, thus eventually offering a technically simple yet effective approach to monitor OPs poisoning and guide the clinical treatment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent sensing of AChE in cerebral spinal fluid
ce:section.ce:para: To demonstrate the validity of our fluorescent method for cerebrospinal AChE sensing, the oxygen-treated CSF sample was then added into the aqueous dispersion of GQDs-MnO2 architecture containing ATCh (100 μM). As shown in Fig. 5 , the oxygen-treated CSF samples leads to an increase of the F 420 value of GQDs in the fluorescent spectra (from blue curve to red curve), which enabled the fluorescent AChE detection. According to the calibration curve discussed above, the initial value of AChE level in CSF of normal rats was detected to be 0.538 ± 0.051 U/mL, which was similar with the values reported in literature before (Liu et al., 2012).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Monitoring the level of AChE in CSF as a biomarker for OPs poisoning and treatment
ce:section.ce:para:
Having demonstrated the validity of our assay for cerebrospinal AChE sensing, we next investigated the possibility of applying this assay for monitoring AChE fluctuation in CSF as a biomarker for OPs poisoning and treatment. Firstly, parathion, one of the typical OPs was chosen as a model. The inhibition efficiencies of the OPs towards AChE was next evaluated by pre-incubating AChE (0.2 U) with the aqueous solutions of different concentrations (i.e., 0, 1, 2.5, 5, 10, 15, 20, 30, 40, 100, 120, 200 ng/mL) of parathion for 20 min. Then the parathion-treated AChE were added into the 1 mL buffer dispersion of the GQDs-MnO2 architecture, respectively. After incubation for an additional 20 min, the resulting mixtures were subjected to the fluorescent measurements. As displayed in Fig. S8, with increasing parathion concentration from 1 ng/mL to 40 ng/mL, the fluorescent intensity of the GQDs-MnO2 dispersions in F
420 decreased linearly ((F− F
0
)/F
0 = 4.700–0.101Cparathion (ng/mL), R2 = 0.996) (Fig. S8B, inset). This is because when the activity of AChE was inhibited by parathion, the ATCh could not be hydrolyzed to reductive TCh anymore, and as a consequence, the GQDs-MnO2 architecture remained unchanged and IFE suppressed the fluorescence of GQDs. Based on this effective inhibition interaction, our assay could potentially be developed as a screening tool for a wealth of OPs in future. Additionally, the IC50 which represents the concentration of an inhibitor that is required for 50% inhibition of an enzyme and always used as a parameter to evaluate the inhibitors was valued. A sigmoidal profile was obtained from the plot of the F
420 values versus the logarithm of parathion concentration, as shown in Fig. S8B. The IC50 value of 0.2 U AChE was thus calculated to be 23.87 ± 1.06 ng/mL, which was consistent with the values reported previously (Eyer and Buckley, 2006; Woreká and Szinicz, 1998), again validating the reliability of the developed fluorescent assay for further application to monitor the level of AChE in CSF as a biomarker for OPs poisoning.

To demonstrate this validity further, rat samples exposed to parathion without and with the treatment of PAM were investigated, respectively. As shown in Fig. 6 A, with the addition of ATCh, the oxygen treated CSF of rat samples exposed to parathion without protection only showed a slight increase of the F 420 value in the fluorescent spectra (from blue curve to red curve). According to the calibration curve, the level of cerebrospinal AChE decreased to 0.103 ± 0.011 U/mL (Table 1 ), which only 20% of the normal group, indicating OPs poisoning inhibited the activity of AChE dramatically, and thus may induce subsequently cholinergic toxicity in central and peripheral nervous system and finally high lethality. In contrast, with the pretreatment/aftertreatment of pralidoxime (PAM), cerebrospinal AChE activity of rat samples exposed to parathion restored, as evidenced by the fluorescence changes of GQDs in Fig. 6B and C (from blue curve to red curve). Also by the calibration curve, the AChE level of the therapeutic samples in PAM-1 and PAM-2 group can be calculated approximately to be 0.402 ± 0.052 U/mL and 0.189 ± 0.054 U/mL, respectively (Table 1). The activation efficiency of the pretreatment PAM-1 group was obvious higher than the after-treatment PAM-2 group.

The result obtained with our assay was consistent with previous report that: as an effective antidote for OPs poisoning, PAM could penetrate across blood–brain barrier and reactivate cerebrospinal cholinesterase by removing the phosphoryl group bound to the esteratic site (Cherian et al., 2005; Eddleston et al., 2009; Kirti et al., 2006; Sakurada et al., 2003). However, this process is time-dependent, the binding of PAM is effective for the newly-formed phosphorus acylation cholinesterase, while the activity of ageing phosphorus acylation cholinesterase (ageing refers to a further chemical reaction of the inhibited enzyme, which completely prevents subsequent reactivation) are hard to be recovered, thus OPs poisoning should be managed with adequate therapy as soon as possible (Eddleston et al., 2005, 2008; Wiilems et al., 1992). To the best of our knowledge, this is the first report in which a MnO2-GQDs based assay with turn-on fluorescence was utilized to monitor AChE fluctuation in CSF samples of rat as a biomarker for OPs poisoning and management.

In this study, by taking advantage of the excellent optical property of GQDs and rationally tailoring the redox reaction between MnO2 and the reductive enzymatic product, we have proposed a turn-on fluorescence assay, which could be employed for the study on OPs poisoning and management with cerebrospinal AChE activity as biomarker directly. This capability is envisaged to be applicable for better understanding the mechanism of brain dysfunctions associate with OPs poisoning and guide protocols for the clinical treatment in a simple and reliable fashion.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research is financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (grants No. 21277048, 21505046 and 21675053), the China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (No. 2016M590336) and the “Chenguang Program” funded by Shanghai Education Development Foundation and Shanghai Municipal Education Commission (No. 15CG21).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the supplementary data related to this article:
Supporting Information Supporting Information

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.envpol.2017.02.024.
